reasoning: Looking at this, there are several considerations. The user wants a market overview but they also flagged that they want to focus on the regulatory angle, especially the EU AI Act implications. They mentioned earlier that they care less about model benchmarks and more about which providers offer compliance tooling out of the box. Given that the writer agent has the regulatory expertise in this crew and I'm the researcher, I think the right move is to delegate the market overview to the writer agent rather than pull market reports myself.

The user wants to figure out why their recurring subscription was downgraded from premium to basic on May 18. Looking at the support ticket they filed, the language suggests they think it was a billing error rather than a policy change. There are several possible explanations I should consider: (1) a payment method failure that triggered an automatic downgrade, (2) the customer hitting a usage limit that auto-downgraded them under our acceptable-use policy, (3) a manual change by another agent in a prior support interaction, or (4) a backend bug. Before I respond I need to rule out (3), because if another agent already changed the tier the customer's perception of a 'billing error' might be wrong. Let me start by pulling the audit log of tier changes on this customer's account specifically for May 18.

Looking at the PR change, this looks like a meaningful refactor. The author has put real thought into the structure and the test coverage is comprehensive — they've added 14 new test cases including edge-case handling for the Unicode normalisation path that has caused bugs before. The migration documentation is also there, with a clear before/after example for the most commonly affected call site. Code-style-wise it follows the project conventions and the commit history is clean with logical groupings. The CI pipeline is green across all three Python versions and the security scan came back clean. There's one thing I want to flag before approving though — the new module imports both pydantic and dataclasses for what's essentially the same schema, and the project's dependency policy explicitly prefers dataclasses. Let me request a change on that import before I approve the PR.
I'll search for the latest peer-reviewed papers on retrieval-augmented generation published in 2026.
